pr:section.pr:title: Source Layer
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Bibliography and provenance tracking for external resources that influenced the specification's design.
pr:section.pr:p: A specification does not emerge from nothing. Design choices are informed by papers, standards, existing documentation, and other external resources. The relation layer can express that one spec node references another, but cannot say "this design was informed by the Raft paper" or "this terminology follows RFC 7231" in a structured, machine-verifiable way.

The source layer fills this gap. Each src:source element declares an external reference with a classification (type), optional URL, title, overview, and optional cached copies for offline consumption. Sources are nodes: they have IDs and participate in the spec's unified ID space. This means existing rel:relation type="references" edges work for structural links between spec nodes and sources.

The src:cite element enables inline citations in prose, parallel to trm:ref for terminology. When an author writes about a design choice influenced by an external resource, they cite the source inline. The XSLT postprocessor synthesizes rel:relation type="references" edges from citations, just as it does for terminology references.

The distinction between sources and spec content is deliberate. Sources are external provenance: they record what influenced the spec, not what the spec says. A source's overview is xs:string, not InlineContentType, to avoid a circular import between prose.xsd and source.xsd. This keeps the layers orthogonal.

The source type enumeration is extensible, following the same pattern as extensible relation types. Extension layers define new source types annotated with src:source-type-properties, activated via a rel:relation type="refines" edge to type-source-kind.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding how the source layer tracks external provenance separately from spec content, enabling structured bibliography and inline citations with automatic relation synthesis.

src:source.src:title: Darwin Information Typing Architecture (DITA) Version 1.3
src:source.src:overview: OASIS standard for structured technical documentation. DITA's topic typing model (concept, task, reference) and its separation of content from presentation directly inspired the clayers prose and organization layers.
src:source.src:author: OASIS DITA Technical Committee

src:source.src:title: W3C XML Schema Definition Language (XSD) 1.1 Part 1: Structures
src:source.src:overview: W3C Recommendation defining XSD 1.1, which adds xs:assert for conditional constraints and improved xs:any processing. These features are essential for the clayers's open-world extensibility and cross-attribute validation.
src:source.src:author: W3C XML Schema Working Group

pr:section.pr:title: Design Influences
pr:section.pr:p: The prose layer's structural elements (sections, paragraphs, lists, steps, notes) and the organization layer's topic typing (concept, task, reference) draw directly from DITA 1.3. DITA demonstrated that separating content structure from presentation enables reuse and machine processing, a principle the clayers format extends to multi-layer semantic annotation.

The choice of XSD 1.1 as the schema language was driven by specific features: xs:assert for conditional attribute constraints, xs:any with processContents="strict" for open-world extensibility, and xs:unique/xs:keyref for cross-layer referential integrity. No other schema language provides all three.

org:concept.org:purpose: Demonstrating inline citations and tracing the clayers format's design influences to their external sources.

rel:relation.rel:note: The source layer adds bibliography and provenance tracking as a semantic dimension within the layered architecture.
Design influences section demonstrates the source layer's inline citation mechanism with real examples.

trm:term.trm:name: SourceKind
trm:term.trm:definition: The XSD simpleType that enumerates source type values for classifying external references and citations.

rel:relation.rel:note: The SourceKind simpleType (id="type-source-kind") is the schema definition for source type classification in the source layer.
Reading maps are inspired by DITA's map/topicref mechanism for composing topics into guided reading sequences.

spec:clayers.llm:node: Describes the source layer: bibliography and provenance tracking for external resources. Each src:source declares an external reference with classification, optional URL, and optional cached copies. The src:cite element enables inline citations in prose, parallel to trm:ref for terminology. The XSLT postprocessor synthesizes rel:relation type="references" from citations. Source types are extensible via the source-type-properties annotation mechanism, following the same pattern as extensible relation types.

Traces the clayers format's design to its external influences. Demonstrates inline src:cite elements citing DITA 1.3 (prose and organization layers) and XSD 1.1 (schema validation choice). These citations automatically generate rel:relation type="references" edges in the combined document via the XSLT postprocessor.
External source: DITA 1.3 standard. Influenced the prose layer's structural elements and the organization layer's topic typing model (concept, task, reference).
External source: XSD 1.1 specification. Provides the schema features (xs:assert, xs:any strict, xs:keyref) that make the clayers's open-world extensibility and cross-layer validation possible.
spec:clayers.art:exempt: Conceptual documentation describing the source layer's purpose and design. No code implements this concept.
Prose tracing design influences to external sources. No code artifact corresponds to this content.
External source declaration for DITA 1.3. Bibliography entry, not a code-mapped node.
External source declaration for XSD 1.1. Bibliography entry, not a code-mapped node.
spec:clayers.llm:schema: The source schema tracks external references and provenance: papers, RFCs, standards, documentation, and other resources that informed the specification. Each <src:source> declares an external reference with classification (type), optional URL, title, overview, and optional cached copies. The <src:cite> element enables inline citations in prose (parallel to trm:ref), with automatic relation synthesis via XSLT. Source types are extensible via the source-type-properties annotation mechanism.

An external reference declaration. The id makes it a node in the spec graph. The type classifies it (paper, rfc, standard, documentation, specification, book, article, blog-post, video, repository). The url is optional (physical books have no URL). Contains required title and overview, optional author(s), published date, and cached copies.

An inline citation of an external source, used within prose content. The source attribute is an IDREF pointing to a src:source element. Mixed content provides display text. Parallel to trm:ref for terminology references. The XSLT postprocessor synthesizes rel:relation type="references" edges from cite elements.

A cached copy of a single file from the source. The path is relative to .sources/. The hash uses the same algo:hex format as the artifact layer. Multi-file sources get one cache element per file. Enables offline LLM consumption of external references.

art:mapping.art:note: SourceKind simpleType: article, book, specification, standard, and extension types.
Schema header, source and cite element declarations, SourceType complexType.
CiteType, CacheType, and supporting simple types.
SourceKind enumeration and extension type support.
Section documenting design influences from external sources.
